title: Intelligence Briefing Dashboard
subtitle: Weekly Conflict Monitor · All Regions
sources: Reuters · AP News · Al Jazeera · ACLED · US Embassy Alerts · LiveUAMap · BNO News · ISW · British Defence Intelligence · Kyiv Post · Sudan Tribune · Irrawaddy · CNN · NBC · CBS · Fox News · HRW · UN News · CFR · Times of Israel · NPR · CNBC · Atlantic Council · Sudan Tribune · US State Dept · OHCHR · Wikipedia · Washington Post

regions.name: Middle East
overview: Iran-Israel war context resets the regional baseline: reporting confirms the US and Israel have been at war with Iran since 28 February 2026, when a wave of nearly 900 US-Israeli strikes killed Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei; a conditional ceasefire has held since 8 April, and Khamenei's state funeral is proceeding 3-9 July across Tehran, Qom, Najaf and Mashhad with his son Mojtaba Khamenei installed as successor. A 17 June Bürgenstock memorandum opened a 60-day US-Iran negotiating window, with Doha technical talks continuing this week even as Iran issued fresh threats over the Strait of Hormuz.

Lebanon framework collapsing in real time: Hezbollah's 'null and void' rejection of the US-brokered framework has been followed by near-daily Israeli strikes and Hezbollah rocket/gunfire exchanges through 3 July, including an Israeli reservist seriously wounded in Bint Jbeil and roughly 10 Hezbollah sites struck in a single operation; President Aoun says the deal 'does not legitimize' continued Israeli presence.

Gaza ceasefire remains a ceasefire in name only: Israeli strikes continued almost daily 28 June-3 July, pushing the post-October-2025-truce death toll above 1,000; Israel has signalled intent to expand territorial control from roughly 60% toward 70% of the Strip, and claims 83% of known Hamas tunnels in IDF-held areas demolished.

Syria shows a mixed trajectory: nationwide lethal violence trended down through late June, but an unclaimed IED bombing in a Damascus café (2 July, 6-9 dead) and the worst Druze-government clashes in months in Suwayda (2-3 July) expose the transitional government's fragile authority even as national numbers improve.

countries.name: Gaza
incidents: 28 Jun — Gaza Strip: Israeli strikes killed 3-4 Palestinians; attacks on displacement tents continued despite the October 2025 ceasefire.
30 Jun — Khan Younis (al-Mawasi): Strikes killed at least 8 people, including a 23-year-old mother and her 1-year-old daughter, when their tent was hit.
1 Jul — Gaza City: Drone strike near a hospital/fuel station killed 3; IDF separately claimed 4 Hamas operatives killed the same day, including a named Shejaiya Battalion security chief.
3 Jul — Gaza (IDF-held areas): Defense Minister Katz claims 83% of known Hamas tunnels demolished; Israel signals intent to expand territorial control toward 70% of the Strip.
countries.coa: Netanyahu's stated intent to push territorial control from roughly 60% toward 70% of Gaza, combined with near-daily lethal strikes on displacement zones, confirms the October 2025 ceasefire is being treated as a platform for continued operations rather than a durable truce. Egyptian-Turkish mediation is intensifying but has not slowed strike tempo. Cumulative post-truce deaths above 1,000 and climbing; humanitarian conditions remain critical with no negotiated resolution pathway in the 90-day window.

countries.name: Lebanon
incidents: 28 Jun — Southern Lebanon: IDF killed a Hezbollah commander and struck numerous targets overnight; Hezbollah Secretary-General Naim Qassem declared the US-brokered framework 'null and void.'
29 Jun–1 Jul — South Lebanon: Continuing Israeli strikes and Hezbollah rocket fire in both directions; Lebanese health ministry reports further deaths.
2 Jul — Beit Yahoun: Israeli bombing operation ignites large fires.

3 Jul — Bint Jbeil, Beit Yahoun, Kounine, Baraachit: IDF strikes roughly 10 Hezbollah sites and a weapons-transfer truck after a Hezbollah gunman seriously wounds an Israeli reservist in Bint Jbeil; separate strike kills a Hezbollah operative emerging from a tunnel on the Ali Taher ridge.
countries.coa: What the prior assessment described as border tension is an active, ongoing war — the US-brokered framework is collapsing under continuous combat rather than surviving as a fragile peace. President Aoun's statement that the deal 'does not legitimize' continued Israeli presence removes any remaining Lebanese government buy-in. Amnesty International warns the deal risks 'betraying war crimes victims.' CRITICAL maintained; renewed full-scale war is the base case absent a diplomatic breakthrough.

countries.name: Iran
incidents: 28-30 Jun — Doha: Qatari officials shuttle between US envoys (Witkoff/Kushner) and Iranian negotiators ahead of technical talks under the 17 June Bürgenstock memorandum.
1 Jul — Doha: US-Iran indirect technical talks address implementing the MoU, including unfreezing roughly $6 billion in Iranian assets; US officials describe talks as 'going well.'
2 Jul — Tehran: Iran's military warns any US interference in the Strait of Hormuz will meet a 'decisive and swift response'; tankers warned to use Tehran-designated routes.

3-4 Jul — Tehran/Qom/Najaf/Mashhad: State funeral proceeds for Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, killed in the 28 February US-Israeli opening strikes of the 2026 Iran war; his son Mojtaba Khamenei has been installed as successor by the Assembly of Experts; 15-20 million mourners and 100+ national delegations expected.
countries.coa: Iran is running two tracks simultaneously: genuine negotiating progress in Doha under the 60-day MoU window, and hardline military signaling over Hormuz, compounded by an unresolved leadership succession following Khamenei's assassination. The funeral period itself creates a pause in talks and a window in which hardliners within the new Mojtaba Khamenei-aligned leadership could harden Iran's negotiating position. CRITICAL maintained; succession consolidation and the 60-day MoU clock are the two indicators to watch.

countries.name: Yemen
incidents: Ongoing — Red Sea: No new Houthi attack, ship seizure, or reactivation confirmed in open-source reporting this week; the Red Sea operational standdown appears to be holding, though this is an absence-of-reporting finding rather than confirmed calm.
Ongoing — Abyan/Shabwa: Unconfirmed reporting of increased AQAP activity; not independently dated to this week.
countries.coa: The Houthi standdown continuing without a new confirmed attack this week is a tentatively positive signal, but should not be read as durable given the broader Iran-Israel war context and unresolved MoU negotiations. HIGH maintained; reactivation risk remains elevated pending the outcome of Doha talks and any shift in the Gaza/Lebanon fronts.

countries.name: Israel
incidents: 30 Jun–1 Jul — Northern Israel: Hezbollah rocket and projectile fire toward Israeli territory continues; no confirmed Israeli civilian casualties identified.
3 Jul — Bint Jbeil security-zone area: Israeli reservist seriously wounded by Hezbollah gunfire in the occupied Lebanese border zone.
3 Jul — Yiron/Avivim: Sirens sound along the northern border, later confirmed false alarms.
countries.coa: Israel's home front saw comparatively few confirmed direct hits this week relative to the Gaza and Lebanon fronts it is actively prosecuting — the most serious casualty was a soldier wounded inside Lebanon rather than an attack on Israeli soil. HIGH maintained; air-defense readiness and monitoring for a shift from false alarms to actual impacts remain the key indicators.

countries.name: Iraq
incidents: Ongoing — Iraq-wide: ISW/Critical Threats reporting characterizes an operational pause by the Islamic Resistance in Iraq, with no new claimed attacks on US facilities this week after 300+ cumulative claimed attacks since 28 February.

Ongoing — Baghdad: PM al-Zaydi's anti-corruption push reportedly meets resistance after Shia Coordination Framework leaders refuse to arrest senior Iranian-backed militia leaders (lower-confidence reporting).
countries.coa: The militia operational pause coincides with the US-Iran Doha negotiating window and represents a tentative de-escalation from recent months. Confidence in this assessment is moderate given limited primary-source access this week. Downgraded to MODERATE; force-protection posture at US facilities should be maintained in case the pause reverses if talks falter.

countries.name: Saudi Arabia
incidents: 2 Jul — Riyadh/Islamabad: Saudi Arabia signs a security-cooperation MOU with Pakistan.
~2 Jul — Strait of Hormuz: Oil-export shipping resumes through Hormuz after a three-month pause tied to the Iran war.
3 Jul — Riyadh: Saudi Arabia participates in UN Counter-Terrorism Week; reports emerge that the US is weighing a troop drawdown from Saudi bases amid post-war posture adjustments.
countries.coa: No confirmed attack or security incident inside Saudi Arabia this week, and resumed Hormuz shipping plus active diplomatic normalization support a genuine, if cautious, de-escalation. Downgraded to MODERATE; latent risk from Houthi or Iran-linked actors persists given Saudi exposure to any reignition of the Iran-Israel war.

countries.name: Syria
incidents: 2 Jul — Damascus (al-Hijaz, near the Palace of Justice): An IED hidden under a café table on al-Nasr Street kills 6-9 and wounds 20+; no group has claimed responsibility.

2-3 Jul — Western Suwayda province: Heavy overnight clashes between Druze National Guard and Syrian Transitional Government forces at Tell Hadid, al-Thaala Road and Umm al-Zaytoun kill at least 2 and injure 50+ — the most serious Suwayda flare-up in months.
Late Jun — Nationwide: Aggregate lethal violence trends down to roughly 13 deaths/week versus 19/week earlier in June, per open-source tracking.
countries.coa: The national de-escalatory trend is undercut by two acute localized breakdowns — an unclaimed Damascus bombing near the seat of government and renewed sectarian violence in Suwayda involving government artillery/drone use despite prior Israeli warnings. HIGH maintained; Druze-government dialogue and accountability for the Damascus bombing are the near-term indicators to watch.

countries.name: Egypt
incidents: 30 Jun — Ankara: Egyptian intelligence chief Hassan Rashad meets his Turkish counterpart to coordinate on Gaza ceasefire phase two and what Cairo calls 'Israel's increasing violations' of the truce.

Ongoing, undated — Sudan border/Red Sea State: Unconfirmed reports of aircraft strikes on artisanal gold-mining sites near the Sudan border (15+ killed); Egyptian government has not confirmed involvement.
countries.coa: Egypt continues its role as an active Gaza ceasefire guarantor alongside Qatar and Turkey, with no major new domestic security incident this week. MODERATE maintained; cross-border friction near Sudan warrants continued monitoring to prevent escalation with Sudanese armed actors.

countries.name: Jordan
incidents: Ongoing — Amman: No new dated security incident identified this week; prior embassy alerts (7 and 11 June) and recurring Syria-border smuggling activity remain the standing risk factors.
countries.coa: A genuinely quiet week in available reporting. MODERATE maintained; latent risk from Gaza/Lebanon-linked unrest potential and Syria-border smuggling networks remains unrealized but unaddressed.

countries.name: Libya
incidents: 29 Jun — Washington: Saddam Haftar meets US Secretary of State Rubio; GNU officials separately meet US officials as UN envoy Hanna Tetteh says the political process 'regains momentum' in a 'narrowing' window.
30 Jun — Sorman: Reported brief detention of an activist by Tripoli's Internal Security Agency (lower-confidence, not independently wire-confirmed).
3 Jul — Tripoli: Fire breaks out at the Internal Security Agency headquarters; investigation opened.
3 Jul — Chad border: Libyan forces detain 74 migrants in ongoing southern border operations.
countries.coa: The US-brokered Haftar-GNU unification track shows renewed momentum this week with no fresh armed militia clashes. MODERATE maintained; underlying Tripoli/Zawiya militia tension remains latent and unresolved.

countries.name: Tunisia
incidents: Ongoing — Gabès: The anti-pollution protest movement (active since October 2025) remains a live grievance with no new dated incident this week.
Ongoing — Tunis: Civil-society crackdown continues as an unresolved legal/political process (court suspension of 25+ associations), not a new incident.
countries.coa: A genuinely quiet week for new security incidents. MODERATE maintained; the Gabès protest movement and civil-society crackdown remain the primary domestic risk factors to monitor.

regions.name: Ukraine/Russia
overview: Ukraine's strike campaign has entered a new phase, and Russia's summer offensive is stalling: Ukrainian deep-strike drones have now hit 8 of Russia's top 10 oil refineries, including repeat strikes on Ufa and a new hit on a major Nizhny Novgorod refinery, driving a domestic Russian fuel crisis Putin has publicly acknowledged for the first time; ISW/CSIS assess Russia at a net territorial loss for a second consecutive month.

Russia responded with its deadliest Kyiv strike in years: the night of 1-2 July saw roughly 74 missiles and 496 drones hit Kyiv and four other regions, killing 27-30 and injuring over 90 in the capital alone, prompting Poland to scramble jets and Finland to restrict airspace — a sign the drone/missile war is generating direct NATO-adjacent spillover risk.

Moldova's pro-EU government suffered a sudden political shock: PM Alexandru Munteanu resigned on 3 July amid a corruption scandal implicating a relative of President Sandu, collapsing the government at the same moment Chișinău shut down the Russian 'Russian House' cultural center and Transnistrian leader Krasnoselsky called the situation 'explosive.'

The South Caucasus continues splitting along a pro-Western/pro-Russian axis: Armenia's Pashinyan defied heavy Russian election interference to win re-election and is accelerating de facto CSTO withdrawal, while Azerbaijan hosted EU Commission President von der Leyen in Baku to advance energy ties and the Armenia peace process, even as Georgian Dream deepens its Moscow-aligned crackdown.

countries.name: Ukraine
incidents: 1-2 Jul — Kyiv: Russia's deadliest strike on the capital in years — roughly 74 missiles (24 ballistic) and 496 drones hit Kyiv and four other regions, killing 27-30 civilians and injuring over 90; residential buildings collapse and rescue operations continue for days.
1 Jul — Kharkiv: Daytime glide-bomb attack (7 bombs) kills a 15-year-old and injures 32 civilians.
1-2 Jul — Kherson/Odesa Oblasts: Russian strikes kill 3 and injure 45 (including 3 children) in Kherson; a ballistic missile strike on Odesa kills 2 and injures 15.

1-2 Jul — Russia (rear): Ukraine strikes Russia's Ufa refinery for the second time in a week and hits a major refinery in Nizhny Novgorod; Zelensky warns 'Moscow will burn' if strikes on Ukraine continue.
countries.coa: Ukraine's deep-strike campaign has now hit 8 of Russia's top 10 oil refineries, driving a domestic Russian fuel crisis Putin has publicly acknowledged for the first time, while Russia responded with its deadliest Kyiv strike in years — prompting Poland to scramble jets and Finland to restrict airspace. ISW/CSIS assess Russia at a net territorial loss for a second consecutive month. CRITICAL maintained; mutual escalation of long-range strikes is the dominant near-term dynamic, with NATO-adjacent airspace spillover an emerging risk.

countries.name: Russia
incidents: 1-2 Jul — Nizhny Novgorod/Ufa: Ukrainian drones strike a major refinery near Moscow's eastern approaches and hit Ufa's refinery for the second time in a week, part of a campaign that has now hit 8 of Russia's top 10 refineries.
1-2 Jul — Kyiv: Conducts its deadliest strike series on Kyiv in years, described by Moscow as retaliation for the refinery strikes and resulting fuel shortages.

Ongoing — Nationwide: Fuel crisis deepens; Putin acknowledges for the first time the impact of Ukrainian strikes on Russian fuel production, following the earlier halt of civilian fuel sales in Crimea.

June (cumulative) — Frontline-wide: Russian forces suffer 39,490 casualties (killed and wounded) in June per Ukrainian General Staff figures; ISW/CSIS assess a second consecutive month of net territorial loss.
countries.coa: Russia's attritional strategy continues at rising cost — refinery losses and fuel rationing are spreading beyond Crimea, raising internal political risk for the Kremlin even as Moscow retaliates with escalating strikes on Ukrainian population centers. HIGH maintained; watch for increased pressure on Belarus for direct military contribution as Russia's own offensive stalls.

countries.name: Belarus
incidents: 1 Jul — Minsk: Lukashenko pardons 28 political prisoners on Independence Day, continuing a pattern tied to Western sanctions-relief outreach; Viasna reports 864 political prisoners remain jailed, including 21 journalists.

Late Jun — Belarus-Ukraine border: Zelensky states Belarus is 'nearing completion' of military infrastructure construction along the Ukrainian border; Belarusian opposition-in-exile warns Kyiv of signs Minsk may be drawn into direct war involvement.
countries.coa: Belarus continues its calibrated dual posture — prisoner releases to sustain Western sanctions-relief talks, while construction along the Ukrainian border raises dual-use concerns. MODERATE maintained; no direct Belarusian combat participation confirmed this week, but border infrastructure warrants continued satellite monitoring.

countries.name: Moldova
incidents: 30 Jun — Chișinău: Moldova signs a contract with Estonia's Center for Defense Investments for 100+ Canadian-made Roshel armored vehicles under a US/EU/NATO-backed modernization push.
3 Jul — Chișinău: PM Alexandru Munteanu resigns abruptly amid a corruption scandal implicating a relative of President Sandu, collapsing the government.
4 Jul — Chișinău: The Russian 'Russian House' cultural/influence center shuts down and removes its flag from its building.
3 Jul — Transnistria: Transnistrian leader Vadim Krasnoselsky calls the situation 'explosive,' accusing Chișinău of abandoning diplomacy — rhetoric amplified by Russian state media.
countries.coa: The sudden government collapse creates a genuine vulnerability window that Russian hybrid/influence operations are already moving to exploit, with Transnistrian rhetoric escalating in parallel. Upgraded to HIGH; Western partners should expedite support for a stable transition and continue the military modernization pipeline regardless of cabinet turnover.

countries.name: Georgia
incidents: 10 Jun — Tbilisi: Activist Zurab Menteshashvili sentenced to nine months for participating in a peaceful protest, part of what Amnesty International calls a 'coordinated system to crush dissent.'
Ongoing — Tbilisi: Pro-EU protests outside parliament continue past the 580-day mark, demanding release of political prisoners including journalist Mzia Amaglobeli.

2 Jul — Tbilisi: PM Kobakhidze publicly urges Washington to relaunch the strategic partnership suspended since November 2024, while EU accession suspension and the 'foreign agents' law remain in place.
Ongoing — US sanctions on Georgian Dream founder Bidzina Ivanishvili remain in force (renewed March 2026).
countries.coa: Georgian Dream is running a dual-track strategy — courting Washington rhetorically while sustaining EU-accession suspension and domestic crackdown. HIGH maintained; Western governments should hold firm on Ivanishvili sanctions and political-prisoner releases as preconditions for any partnership relaunch.

countries.name: Armenia
incidents: 7 Jun — Nationwide: Pashinyan's Civil Contract party wins parliamentary election with 49.81% despite reported heavy Russian covert interference; opposition alleges fraud and calls for annulment (12 Jun, still unresolved).
Ongoing — Trade: Russia maintains suspension of Armenian seafood, flower, mineral water and alcohol imports as economic retaliation for Yerevan's Western pivot.
Ongoing — Yerevan: Parliament speaker states Armenia has 'effectively withdrawn' from the CSTO, though Russian troops remain on Armenian territory.
countries.coa: Armenia's Western pivot continues to solidify despite Russian election interference and economic retaliation. MODERATE maintained, improving trajectory; watch for further Russian economic coercion and the pro-Russian opposition's annulment campaign for signs of escalation into street mobilization.

countries.name: Azerbaijan
incidents: 1 Jul — Baku: European Commission President von der Leyen visits, congratulating Aliyev on the Armenia peace agreement and discussing energy cooperation and a Global Gateway pledge of up to €200 million for South Caucasus infrastructure.
1 Jul — New York: Aliyev raises a demand at the UN to resettle 300,000 ethnic Azeris in Armenia, a sharp claim that could complicate the peace process's optics.
Ongoing — Border: The Armenia border remains formally closed; delimitation talks continue, with roughly 12 km already delimited.
countries.coa: No clashes, ceasefire violations, or military incidents this week — the notable developments are diplomatic and economic. Downgraded to LOW; sustained EU energy engagement should be used to keep Baku anchored to the Western-mediated peace track, while pressing Baku to moderate maximalist UN rhetoric that could unsettle the fragile process.

regions.name: Sahel
overview: El Obeid (Sudan) has become the region's single most acute atrocity-risk flashpoint: a 26 June UN Security Council briefing and a 3 July HRW statement both explicitly invoked the El Fasher massacre precedent, with ACLED recording a record 27 drone-strike events in June alone and RSF now controlling all routes into the city except the eastern approach.

Jihadist groups across the Sahel are increasingly targeting capital-adjacent strategic infrastructure rather than only rural garrisons: Niger's Niamey airport was breached for the second time in 2026 on 18 June, while JNIM's fuel blockade continues to visibly darken Bamako at night.

Nigeria's security crisis is bifurcating into two simultaneous, geographically distinct fronts: ISWAP/Boko Haram attacks on military infrastructure and schools in Borno (a 29 June school abduction left 36+ students still captive), and a separate, self-sustaining cycle of herder-farmer retaliatory violence in Benue's Middle Belt.

Information gaps are themselves a strategic signal: Burkina Faso, Chad and Mauritania each show a near-absence of freshly dated, sourced reporting this week despite ongoing or recently acute crises — for Burkina Faso in particular this is plausibly explained by internet blackouts and journalist detentions already noted, meaning the absence of news should be read as continued crisis-level opacity, not de-escalation.

countries.name: Mali
incidents: Ongoing since Sept 2025, continuing this week — Bamako approaches: JNIM's fuel blockade (300+ tankers burned) continues to strangle the capital; satellite imagery shows Bamako visibly darker at night amid rationing prioritizing military/government use.
1 Jun — Bamako-Kayes highway: A JNIM landmine strikes a passenger bus, killing 8 and injuring 42.
Ongoing this week — Bamako approaches: Russian Africa Corps and Turkish drone support fail to break the blockade; convoy interdiction on supply routes into the capital continues.
countries.coa: No sign of blockade resolution this week — JNIM's siege-by-attrition of Bamako's fuel supply lines remains the central strategic threat, with Russian and Turkish support unable to break the encirclement. CRITICAL maintained; this is now sustained siege warfare rather than episodic attacks.

countries.name: Niger
incidents: 18 Jun — Niamey, Diori Hamani International Airport: JNIM commandos (including suicide bombers) infiltrate via taxis/trucks, killing 13 and losing 22 attackers; 20 suspects arrested. Second attack on this airport in 2026, which hosts G5 Sahel and Russian forces.
Ongoing — Tillabéri region: Continued JNIM and ISSP activity; Tillabéri remains the deadliest area in the central Sahel for civilians.
countries.coa: The June 18 Niamey airport attack marked a significant capability escalation onto a capital strategic asset; this week's activity is largely consolidation and investigation following that attack plus persistent low-level Tillabéri violence. HIGH maintained; no new mass-casualty event confirmed this week, but capital-reach capability by JNIM is now demonstrated.

countries.name: Burkina Faso
incidents: Ongoing — Dori: Continued siege conditions consistent with prior MSF hospital-capacity collapse and 5-district operational suspension; no independently verified new dated incident found this week, likely reflecting the same internet blackouts and journalist detentions suppressing outward news flow.
countries.coa: The near-total absence of fresh sourced reporting this week is very likely an information blackout effect rather than genuine de-escalation — Dori's internet restrictions and journalist detentions would themselves suppress news flow. CRITICAL maintained on the sustained structural crisis; satellite imagery and IDP-flow data in neighboring countries should be prioritized to compensate for the reporting gap.

countries.name: Sudan
incidents: 26 Jun — UN Security Council, New York: UN political affairs chief DiCarlo warns an escalation in El Obeid would place 'hundreds of thousands of civilians at immediate risk'; new US sanctions announced over chemical-weapons-related procurement networks.

June (cumulative) — El Obeid/Sheikan locality: ACLED records 27 drone-strike events in June alone, the highest monthly total of the war; UN documents 15 strikes in three weeks killing at least 45 civilians, hitting markets, schools, and water infrastructure.
1 Jul — Tendelti, White Nile state: A UNHCR relief truck carrying 50 metric tonnes of aid is destroyed in a drone strike.

3 Jul — Geneva: UN Human Rights Commissioner Volker Türk warns El Obeid faces 'relentless' drone attacks, explicitly invoking the El Fasher massacre precedent; HRW publishes an urgent atrocity-prevention call for a civilian protection mission and expanded arms embargo.
countries.coa: Record-high June drone-strike tempo, an explicit UN Security Council warning, and a 3 July HRW atrocity alert place El Obeid at the highest documented risk level of any locality in this briefing — RSF now controls all routes into the city except the eastern approach. CRITICAL maintained; warnings have produced only incremental sanctions with no enforcement mechanism or halt to the drone campaign. If El Obeid falls, RSF gains the Kordofan-Darfur corridor.

countries.name: Chad
incidents: Context (4-7 May) — Barka Tolorom, Lake Chad: Boko Haram attack on a military base/island killed 23-26 soldiers including two generals; Chad declared a 20-day state of emergency, since expired. No new dated incident confirmed this week.
countries.coa: No new confirmed incident this week; Chad remains at HIGH reflecting the still-unresolved Boko Haram/ISWAP contest for Lake Chad basin territory following May's emergency declaration. Continued multinational coordination against both factions is warranted, alongside investment in early-warning reporting to close the current information gap.

countries.name: Mauritania
incidents: Context — Mali border: JNIM activity on the Malian side of the border continues (recent cross-border incidents in Hodh el-Gharbi); no dated incident inside Mauritania itself found this week.
countries.coa: Direct domestic incidents remain rare; Mauritania continues to stave off cross-border spillover from Mali's deepening crisis with continued US-backed border security cooperation. MODERATE maintained.

countries.name: Nigeria
incidents: 29 Jun — Borno State: Islamist militants strike a secondary school, killing 2 teachers and abducting 45 students and staff; 36 students and 1 staff member remain captive.

1 Jul — Guzamala LGA, Borno State (FOB Mairari): ISWAP attacks a Forward Operating Base at a resettled community; Operation HADIN KAI troops repel the attack, killing scores of insurgents; Nigeria loses 2 soldiers and 1 CJTF member.

1 Jul — Sai community, Katsina-Ala LGA, Benue State: Suspected armed herdsmen kill 10 and injure 15+, razing houses; believed linked to retaliation for a 26 June killing of a Miyetti Allah herder leader.
Ongoing — Borno: A cholera outbreak (since May) has killed at least 74, compounding the security crisis's humanitarian toll.
countries.coa: Nigeria faces continued high-tempo activity on two simultaneous fronts — ISWAP/Boko Haram attacks on military and civilian targets in Borno, and intercommunal retaliatory violence in Benue's Middle Belt. The unresolved school abduction (36+ still captive) is the most acute single incident. CRITICAL maintained; sustained operational pressure and rescue negotiations are both required.

regions.name: Asia-Pacific
overview: Myanmar's civil war is entering an endgame phase in the west: the Arakan Army now controls roughly 14 of 17 Rakhine townships and has pushed into neighboring Ayeyarwady Region, while also effectively encircling the China-backed Kyaukphyu SEZ/port — testing whether Chinese patronage can protect its $1.3B Bay of Bengal investment.

China is running simultaneous grey-zone pressure campaigns on three fronts: a rare naval standoff with a Philippine frigate near Scarborough Shoal, a second Fujian carrier transit of the Taiwan Strait, a joint China-Russia bomber patrol entering South Korea's ADIZ, and new export-control sanctions on 20 Japanese entities deepening the Japan-China diplomatic crisis.

The Afghanistan-Pakistan border war escalated into Taliban drone strikes on Pakistani soil for the first time: on 1 July Afghan forces launched drone strikes into Pakistan's Balochistan and conducted airstrikes in Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, compounding Pakistan's separate internal militancy crisis (a 27 June Karachi Rangers camp bombing).

Indonesia's Papua insurgency crossed a new threshold with the killing of a foreign national: on 3 July, Indonesian authorities recovered the body of American pilot Nicholas Goselin, shot by West Papua Liberation Army (TPNPB) fighters who then burned his aircraft — the first confirmed killing of a US citizen in the decades-old conflict.

countries.name: North Korea
incidents: 25-26 Jun — DPRK: Kim Jong Un oversees new multiple rocket launcher and ballistic missile warhead tests marking the Korean War anniversary.

Late Jun/early Jul — DMZ: South Korea's Defense Ministry raises concerns with the UN Command after detecting North Korean troops expanding DMZ fortification work, including fencing and mine-laying, within 100m of the Military Demarcation Line.
27 Jun — Pyongyang: Kim sends a congratulatory message to Xi Jinping marking the CCP's 105th founding anniversary, reaffirming closer alignment with Beijing.
countries.coa: Sustained weapons-testing cadence combined with active DMZ fortification and mine-laying keeps tension elevated even absent a new missile launch this week. CRITICAL maintained; enhanced ISR coverage of DMZ activity and continued joint ROK-US monitoring of test cadence are warranted.

countries.name: Myanmar
incidents: 1 Jul — Rakhine State: The Arakan Army launches the 'Arakan Lottery,' a formal revenue-generating state institution, signaling quasi-state permanence.
Late Jun/early Jul — Ayeyarwady Region: Fighting intensifies in Lemyethna, Yegyi and Thabaung townships as AA and allies push along the Gwa-Ngathaingchaung Road; roughly 4,000 civilians displaced.
Ongoing — Kyaukphyu: AA advances to within about 5km of the China-backed SEZ/port/pipeline hub, effectively surrounding it; roughly 50 Chinese security personnel remain on-site.
countries.coa: Junta control in Rakhine is nearly collapsed and fighting is metastasizing into Ayeyarwady Region; China's flagship $1.3B Kyaukphyu investment is now under direct military threat. CRITICAL maintained; watch for an AA assault on or negotiated transfer of Kyaukphyu and Chinese contingency planning for its remaining personnel.

countries.name: Pakistan
incidents: 27 Jun — Karachi (Gulistan-i-Jauhar): Jamaat-ul-Ahrar militants attack a Pakistan Rangers camp with a VBIED and gunfire; 3 paramilitary soldiers killed, 4 injured; an Afghan national is captured and alleges Afghan-based training.

1 Jul — Balochistan (Saranan)/Khyber Pakhtunkhwa: Afghan Taliban forces launch drone strikes into Pakistani territory and conduct airstrikes in KPK — the first confirmed direct Taliban aerial assault on Pakistan; two civilians injured near a school; Pakistan says it downed four drones.
Ongoing — Durand Line: Continuation of the broader Afghanistan-Pakistan border war active since February 2026, with hundreds killed to date and mediation stalled.
countries.coa: The 1 July Taliban drone strikes mark a significant escalation of the border conflict into a state-vs-state aerial dimension, compounding Pakistan's separate domestic militancy crisis in Karachi and Balochistan. Upgraded to CRITICAL; air-defense/counter-drone posture along the Durand Line and renewed China/Qatar/Gulf-mediated talks with Kabul are priorities.

countries.name: India
incidents: Late Jun — Beijing: The 35th Working Mechanism for Consultation and Coordination meeting on India-China border affairs continues the post-2025 thaw on delimitation and boundary management.
Ongoing — Kashmir/LAC: No major incident confirmed this week; the India-Pakistan ceasefire (since May 2025) and India-China LAC de-escalation both continue to hold.
countries.coa: No acute incidents this week; LAC diplomacy continues constructively even as underlying India-Pakistan and India-China frictions remain unresolved. MODERATE maintained.

countries.name: China
incidents: 27 Jun — Sea of Japan/East China Sea: China conducts its 11th joint strategic bomber patrol with Russia, entering South Korea's ADIZ and prompting ROK fighter scrambles.
Early Jul — Taiwan Strait: The Fujian carrier transits the strait for a second time since 23 June, timed against Taiwan's five-day combat readiness drill.
2 Jul — Beijing: MOFCOM places 20 Japanese entities on an export control list and 20 more on a dual-use watch list, citing Japanese 'militarism.'
Late Jun — Scarborough Shoal: Four Chinese warships confront the Philippine frigate BRP Diego Silang in a rare naval standoff.
countries.coa: China is running simultaneous grey-zone pressure across the Taiwan Strait, South China Sea, Yellow Sea, and East China Sea/Japan fronts. HIGH maintained; no major military action against Taiwan expected in the 90-day window, but multi-theater intensity is increasing, and the PLA's internal ideological/anti-corruption campaign warrants monitoring for command-stability implications.

countries.name: Taiwan
incidents: Late Jun — Taiwan-wide: Conducts a five-day 'Immediate Combat Readiness Exercise' emphasizing rapid mobilization against grey-zone/sudden-attack scenarios.
Early Jul — Taiwan Strait: The Fujian carrier's transit is closely monitored by Taiwan's MND; its empty flight deck is assessed as a lower-threat signal.
Late Jun/early Jul — Washington: Legislative Yuan Speaker Han Kuo-yu leads a multipartisan delegation to meet US Congress members on international participation, trade, and security.
countries.coa: Continuous PLA pressure paired with Taiwan's own readiness escalation reflects a stable but tense standoff. HIGH maintained; the mid-July Joint Defense Exercise and August Han Kuang drills are the near-term indicators to watch.

countries.name: Philippines
incidents: Late Jun — Scarborough Shoal: Four Chinese warships confront the Philippine Navy frigate BRP Diego Silang in a rare direct naval standoff; incident concludes without kinetic escalation.
Jun — Scarborough Shoal: China reportedly removes its floating barrier following Philippine diplomatic protest, shortly before the naval standoff occurs.

Ongoing — South China Sea: The 14th Philippines-US-Australia Maritime Cooperative Activity patrol is conducted; the 'Salaknib 2026' exercise (US-Philippines-Japan-Australia-NZ, ~7,000 troops) concludes after nearly three months.
countries.coa: The direct four-ship naval confrontation marks a notable escalation in Chinese assertiveness even without shots fired or collisions. Upgraded to HIGH; continued multilateral patrols and ISR-sharing with Japan/Australia/US, plus diplomatic protest through ASEAN channels, are the recommended posture.

countries.name: South Korea
incidents: 27 Jun — South Korea ADIZ: A joint China-Russia bomber patrol (9 aircraft) enters the ADIZ; ROK Air Force scrambles fighters; no airspace violation confirmed.
28 Jun — Seoul: Formal diplomatic protests lodged with both Beijing and Moscow over the ADIZ incursion.
1-3 Jul — Ankara/Ulaanbaatar: President Lee Jae Myung visits the NATO Summit and Mongolia to promote K-defense exports and diversify partnerships.
countries.coa: Combined China-Russia signaling plus continued North Korean DMZ fortification activity raises South Korea's risk profile modestly. MODERATE maintained; sustained ADIZ response protocols and diplomatic protest mechanisms are warranted.

countries.name: Vietnam
incidents: Ongoing — Spratly Islands: Vietnamese construction continues at 27 sites across 18+ reefs (~2,771 acres reclaimed cumulatively), including a roughly 13,000-ft runway under construction at Barque Canada Reef.
Ongoing (12-month data) — South China Sea: Chinese vessel transits within 10nm of contested Vietnamese-held features rise to 89 over the past year, up from 50 three years prior.
countries.coa: Structural tension via competing island-building continues to rise, but no acute confrontation occurred this week. MODERATE maintained; satellite monitoring of construction pace on both sides is the key indicator.

countries.name: Japan
incidents: 27 Jun — Near Japan: The China-Russia joint bomber patrol transits the Sea of Japan/East China Sea/western Pacific.

2 Jul — Beijing: China's MOFCOM imposes new export-control sanctions on 20 Japanese entities plus 20 more on a dual-use watch list, in direct retaliation tied to the ongoing China-Japan diplomatic crisis stemming from PM Takaichi's November 2025 Taiwan remarks.
Ongoing — Senkaku Islands: Japanese fishers continue to be advised to avoid Senkaku waters amid persistent Chinese Coast Guard presence.
countries.coa: New economic sanctions represent a concrete escalation atop the unresolved diplomatic crisis and joint military signaling near Japanese waters. Upgraded to MODERATE; assess retaliatory options on the export controls and continue coordinated ADIZ monitoring with South Korea.

countries.name: Indonesia
incidents: 3 Jul — Ipdeheik airstrip, Yahukimo regency, Papua Highlands: American pilot Nicholas Goselin is shot dead by West Papua Liberation Army (TPNPB) fighters, who then burn his aircraft; seven Indigenous Papuan passengers are unharmed — the first confirmed killing of a US citizen in the decades-old conflict.
Ongoing — Papua: TPNPB spokesman Sebby Sambom claims responsibility and threatens further attacks on aircraft it deems supportive of Indonesian military operations.
Context — Papua: Indonesia has tripled its troop deployment (now roughly 83,000 soldiers/police) amid a marked spike in insurgency-related killings over the past year.
countries.coa: The first killing of a US citizen in the Papua conflict raises the international profile and risk of foreign-national targeting significantly. Upgraded to HIGH; expect increased US diplomatic engagement, monitor for further TPNPB attacks on civilian/charter aircraft, and track any shift in Indonesian military posture in Yahukimo regency.

countries.name: Thailand
incidents: Ongoing — Thailand-Cambodia border: The ceasefire in effect since 27 December 2025 continues to hold; only diplomatic friction reported this week (Cambodia protesting delay of Joint Boundary Commission talks), no new violence.
countries.coa: Ceasefire holding but underlying mistrust and a stalled Joint Boundary Commission process leave residual risk. LOW maintained; resumption of JBC talks is the key indicator to monitor.

countries.name: Bangladesh
incidents: Ongoing — Dhaka: No major dated incident this week; continued political volatility under the BNP government following February 2026 elections and unresolved reform commitments from the 2024 uprising remain the standing concern.
countries.coa: A genuinely quiet week. LOW maintained; monitor for renewed Awami League-BNP/NCP friction and reform implementation.

countries.name: Sri Lanka
incidents: Ongoing — Colombo: Sri Lanka deploys 1,000+ military/police personnel to Haiti's Gang Suppression Force, amid unresolved allegations of past misconduct by Sri Lankan peacekeepers raised by Amnesty International.
Jun — Colombo: UN Human Rights Commissioner Volker Türk visits and calls for accountability reforms on conflict-era abuses.
countries.coa: No domestic security incident this week; the primary story is international (Haiti deployment controversy) rather than a domestic threat. LOW maintained.

regions.name: Americas
overview: Colombia's post-election security transition is the region's sharpest new flashpoint: with the ELN's election ceasefire expired (23 June) and confirmed follow-on violence — a 13-injury bombing at an Arauca oil complex and a five-department 'armed strike' (3-6 July) — the ELN appears to be pre-positioning for confrontation with incoming President De la Espriella, who takes office 7 August on an explicitly anti-'total peace' platform.

Peru's razor-thin presidential resolution adds a second major electoral-legitimacy crisis to the region: Keiko Fujimori was confirmed winner on 3 July by roughly 50,000 votes out of 18.4 million — one of the closest results in recent Latin American history — with unrest risk concentrated in Ayacucho over historical protester killings.

Cuba and Haiti remain the region's two deepest humanitarian emergencies, both worsening in parallel: Cuba's US-enforced oil blockade has driven record protest levels (109 in June, the largest wave since 2021) alongside collapsing food/medicine/infant-mortality indicators; Haiti's Gang Suppression Force remains stuck at roughly 25% of mandated strength while UN reporting documents systemic sexual violence and large-scale child recruitment by gangs.

Venezuela's stabilization narrative held steady this week but rests on a fragile, incompletely-verified foundation: oil-sector normalization continued even as the transitional authority absorbed a natural disaster (24 June earthquakes, still disrupting Caracas air travel and power/internet into July), with no clearly dated Maduro-loyalist sabotage incident confirmed in open sources this week.

countries.name: Venezuela
incidents: 24 Jun (context)/28 Jun-1 Jul — Nationwide: Recovery continues from 24 June earthquakes off the northern coast; Simón Bolívar International Airport remains closed to commercial traffic into early July, with rolling power/internet outages complicating the transitional government's stabilization efforts.
Late Jun/early Jul — Oil sector: The SLB-PDVSA framework agreement and eased US Treasury general licenses continue driving recovery, with output tracking toward 1.3 million bpd in 2026.

Ongoing — No confirmed, specifically-dated Maduro-loyalist sabotage incident was identified this week; treat as a possible reporting gap rather than a confirmed lull, given historical underreporting of loyalist cell activity.
countries.coa: Natural-disaster response is now the dominant near-term risk driver rather than loyalist sabotage, while institutional and economic stabilization (oil exports, sanctions relief, 47-state recognition) continues advancing. Downgraded to HIGH; maintain vigilance against residual Maduro-loyalist sabotage cells despite this week's absence of confirmed incidents, and expedite earthquake-related infrastructure assistance to lock in the stabilization trajectory.

countries.name: Haiti
incidents: 16 Jun (context, still driving coverage) — Port-au-Prince: UN Secretary-General Guterres tours the Gang Suppression Force base at Vertières; Jamaica, Chad, El Salvador and Guatemala have contributed under 1,000 troops combined toward the 5,500 mandate.

Ongoing — Port-au-Prince: An estimated 26+ gangs still control up to 90% of the capital; UN reporting documents 2,088 grave violations against 1,661 children in 2025 (892 recruited into combat roles) and at least 291 women/girls subjected to sexual violence.
Upcoming (flagged) — 14 Jul, New York/Port-au-Prince: UN Security Council slated for its 90-day Haiti briefing on GSF progress and a proposed UN-backed DDR trust fund.
countries.coa: GSF remains critically understaffed at roughly 25% of mandated strength and gangs continue to hold near-total territorial control of the capital. CRITICAL maintained; press troop-contributing nations to accelerate force generation ahead of the 14 July Security Council briefing, and prioritize child-soldier extraction and protection of women and girls given the scale of documented violence.

countries.name: Colombia
incidents: 23 Jun — Arauca/Norte de Santander: The ELN's pre-election ceasefire expires as scheduled, ending the last vestige of the 'total peace' framework's restraint.
~2 Jul — Arauca: ELN detonates explosives at an oil company complex, seriously injuring at least 13 people; Defense Minister Pinzón condemns it as 'an act of absolute cowardice.'
3-6 Jul — Arauca, Boyacá, Casanare, Santander, Norte de Santander: ELN declares a multi-department 'armed strike' marking its 50th anniversary, threatening businesses and blocking roads.

22 Jun (context) — Nationwide: Abelardo De la Espriella is confirmed winner of the presidential runoff (49.7%), campaigning to end 'total peace' and pursue a security-first approach; takes office 7 August.
countries.coa: The Arauca oil-complex bombing and five-department armed strike are the clearest evidence yet that the ELN is moving into open confrontation ahead of the political transition. HIGH maintained; expect intensified ELN activity in Arauca/Norte de Santander/Catatumbo through the 7 August transition, with enhanced protection of energy infrastructure a near-term priority.

countries.name: Cuba
incidents: ~24-26 Jun — Havana (El Cerro, El Vedado, Regla): Wave of pot-banging protests over gas/electricity outages affecting 208,000-284,000 customers across eight municipalities; regime response includes militarizing affected neighborhoods.
26 Jun — Havana: Seven individuals, including four minors, detained during blackout protests.

Late Jun/ongoing — Santiago de Cuba and eastern provinces: Protests continue spreading, described by analysts as the largest wave of unrest since July 2021; Cubalex records 109 protests in June alone.
Ongoing — Nationwide: ETECSA confirms backup batteries last only hours, with blackouts disabling roughly 47.5% of mobile base stations and 56.5% of telecom cabinets nationwide.
countries.coa: The convergence of the US oil-tanker blockade, collapsing food and medicine supply, and record-breaking protest frequency puts Cuba at its highest instability level of the crisis to date. Upgraded to CRITICAL; monitor for potential inflection toward a broader nationwide uprising akin to July 2021, particularly if blackout durations lengthen into August heat.

countries.name: Ecuador
incidents: Ongoing this week — Guayaquil: Shooting/attack at Guayaquil airport; two minors (15-16) detained with firearms after a shootout in the terminal, roughly 24 hours after the government declared a state of emergency across 10 provinces.
Context (H1 2026 data) — Nationwide: Ecuador records 4,600+ homicides in the first half of 2026, a 47% year-on-year increase, a third consecutive year as the region's murder capital.

Ongoing — Prison system: The Chone Killers gang, recently US-designated as a terrorist organization, fragments into at least 10 subgroups following leadership arrests, with splinters expanding into new territory.
countries.coa: The Guayaquil airport shooting marks a notable escalation onto transport infrastructure, and the homicide trajectory continues worsening despite the militarized crackdown. Upgraded to HIGH; track whether the 10-province state of emergency reduces the homicide rate or merely displaces violence, and harden transport hubs against further high-visibility attacks.

countries.name: Peru
incidents: 3 Jul — Nationwide: Keiko Fujimori is officially declared winner of the presidential runoff by an extremely narrow margin (50.135% to 49.865%, roughly 50,000 votes out of 18.4 million) after nearly a month of vote-count delays and fraud allegations.

Ongoing — Ayacucho and Andean regions: General strikes and protests continue over anger at Popular Force's historical defense of security-force killings of 49 protesters during 2022-23 unrest; Ayacucho gave Fujimori only 21% support versus 79% for the runner-up.
Context — Lima: Runner-up Rafael López Aliaga, who called for an 'insurgency' over alleged first-round fraud, faces criminal incitement charges — a second axis of post-election tension.
countries.coa: One of the narrowest presidential victories in the region's recent history, layered onto pre-existing anger over the 2022-23 protester killings, creates meaningful near-term unrest risk. Upgraded to HIGH; monitor southern Andean regions for escalation around Fujimori's 28 July inauguration, with two distinct protest movements (left and right) now running in parallel.

countries.name: Bolivia
incidents: 23 Jun (context) — Nationwide: Government declares all major road blockades cleared after seven weeks of paralyzing protests, following President Paz's state-of-emergency decree authorizing military-assisted road clearance.
Late Jun — Nationwide: Clashes between protesters and police/military during road-clearing operations; at least one person killed in unclear circumstances per HRW.

2 Jul — Nationwide: HRW publishes 'Bolivia Cleared the Roads, but it Hasn't Cleared the Crisis,' warning the underlying economic grievances (fuel-subsidy elimination, dollar shortage, exclusion of Indigenous/labor sectors) remain unresolved.
countries.coa: Physical unrest has genuinely subsided, but HRW's 2 July report explicitly warns this is a lull, not a resolution. MODERATE maintained; press the Paz government toward genuine stakeholder inclusion rather than relying on militarized enforcement, and monitor fuel/dollar-shortage indicators as the leading predictor of a fresh protest wave.

countries.name: Dominican Republic
incidents: Ongoing — Dajabón: Border trade remains restricted due to Haitian gang advance in the Artibonite zone; security forces maintain heightened vigilance.
Ongoing — Santo Domingo: Government continues biweekly deportation operations of irregular migrants amid extended Haiti border closures.
countries.coa: Dominican Republic faces Haiti spillover risk but maintains internal security stability. LOW maintained; the primary monitoring requirement remains Haitian gang advance toward the border zone.

countries.name: Jamaica
incidents: 1-3 Jul — Richmond, St Mary: A 48-hour curfew is imposed in response to localized violence/gang activity.
H1 2026 data — Nationwide: Serious crime falls 19.5% nationally versus H1 2025 (murders -22.5%, shootings -27.7%), though officials note sharp parish-level contrasts.
countries.coa: National crime statistics show genuine improvement, but the St Mary curfew demonstrates continued localized violent hotspots. LOW maintained; direct targeted resources to underperforming parishes bucking the national trend.
